text:
HEPATOPANCREATICOBILIARY SURGERY — POST-OPERATIVE FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Pylorus-Preserving Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple Procedure)
Visit Type: Post-Operative Follow-Up, approximately 4 weeks after surgery
Attending Surgeon: [HPB Surgical Oncology]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African-descent female presenting for her 4-week post-operative follow-up after pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) performed for borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process. The patient was discharged from the hospital on postoperative day 18 with a surgical drain in place and is now returning for wound assessment, drain management review, and surgical pathology discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a retired high school science teacher with a background of type 2 diabetes mellitus (now further complicated by pancreatogenic endocrine insufficiency), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. She initially presented approximately seven months ago with a 3-month history of epigastric pain radiating to the back, 14-pound weight loss, and worsening glycemic control. Cross-sectional imaging at that time demonstrated a 3.4 cm hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process with SMA abutment greater than 180 degrees of contact and SMV narrowing with approximately 40% luminal compromise, consistent with borderline resectable disease by NCCN criteria. Biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CK7+, CK19+, MUC1+, p53 aberrant overexpression, SMAD4 retained). CA 19-9 at diagnosis was 1,200 U/mL. Next-generation sequencing demonstrated KRAS G12V mutation, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and RNF43 frameshift; microsatellite stable, TMB-low; no actionable fusions or BRCA/PALB2/ATM alterations.

She was treated with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, irinotecan 150 mg/m2 reduced to 120 mg/m2 after grade 2 diarrhea, 5-FU 2,400 mg/m2 over 46 hours, every 2 weeks) for 8 cycles over approximately 4 months. Restaging after 8 cycles showed a reduction in tumor size to 2.6 cm with significant CA 19-9 decline to 85 U/mL (93% reduction). SMA abutment decreased to approximately 120 degrees. Given persistent borderline SMA involvement, the multidisciplinary tumor board recommended long-course chemoradiation prior to surgical exploration. She received 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions via IMRT with concurrent capecitabine (825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days), completed over approximately 5.5 weeks. Post-chemoradiation restaging CT demonstrated further tumor reduction to 2.3 cm with SMA abutment estimated at 80-90 degrees, CA 19-9 declined to 52 U/mL.

She subsequently underwent pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with regional lymphadenectomy approximately 8 weeks after completing chemoradiation. Operative findings were favorable: no peritoneal or hepatic surface metastases; intraoperative frozen section of the SMA margin was negative; portal vein/SMV reconstruction was not required. Estimated blood loss was 450 mL. She was transferred to the surgical ICU postoperatively in stable condition.

Postoperative course was complicated by:
- Grade B pancreatic fistula (ISGPF grade B), diagnosed on postoperative day 6 based on elevated drain fluid amylase (greater than 3x upper limit of normal on POD 3), managed conservatively with maintenance of the surgical drain, NPO status, total parenteral nutrition (TPN), and octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneously three times daily. The drain remained in place at the time of discharge.
- Grade A delayed gastric emptying, requiring nasogastric tube decompression, resolved by approximately postoperative day 14.
- The patient was discharged on postoperative day 18 with the surgical drain in place and instructions for drain care.

Final surgical pathology from the Whipple specimen demonstrated: moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, 2.1 cm (reduced from 3.4 cm at diagnosis), with College of American Pathologists pathologic treatment response grade 2 (moderate response, estimated 40% viable tumor, 60% fibrosis/acellular mucin pools). R0 resection confirmed (closest margin: uncinate/SMA margin at 3 mm). Two of 18 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (pN1). Lymphovascular invasion present; extensive perineural invasion present. Pathologic stage: pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition).

She now presents at 4 weeks post-operatively for follow-up. She reports that she has been managing the surgical drain at home with assistance from her daughter. Over the past week, drain output has been decreasing substantially in both volume and character (less turbid, less amber-colored). She continues to have mild incisional discomfort at the midline abdominal wound, which she rates 2/10 and is managed with acetaminophen. She reports a gradual improvement in appetite and has been tolerating a low-fat, small frequent meal diet. She has had no fevers, chills, or rigors at home. She denies nausea or vomiting. She continues to have loose stools (2-3 per day), which is expected given her anatomy post-Whipple. She has been compliant with pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (pancrelipase; PERT) with meals and snacks, though she notes that stool consistency has not fully normalized. Blood glucose monitoring at home has shown values ranging from 140-220 mg/dL fasting; she is currently on insulin (regimen adjusted by endocrinology), and her glucose control remains a work in progress.

She denies worsening abdominal pain, increased drain output, erythema around the drain or wound, signs of wound dehiscence, jaundice, dark urine, clay-colored stool, or new neurologic symptoms. She has not required any emergency department visits since discharge.

Weight at discharge: she had lost approximately 4 lbs during chemoradiation and had additional postoperative weight loss; overall, she estimates she is down approximately 18 lbs from her pre-diagnosis baseline. She has been taking oral nutritional supplements (Ensure Plus) between meals.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, head/uncinate process — now status post pylorus-preserving Whipple procedure; pathologic stage pT3 pN1 M0 (Stage IIB), R0 resection
2. Post-pancreatectomy exocrine pancreatic insufficiency — on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy
3. Post-pancreatectomy endocrine insufficiency (pancreatogenic diabetes, now requiring insulin) — pre-existing type 2 DM now markedly worsened
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 10 years prior; prior therapy was metformin (now off metformin given pancreatogenic DM and renal considerations); now on insulin
5. Hypertension — managed with amlodipine
6. Hyperlipidemia — on atorvastatin
7. Grade B pancreatic fistula, postoperative — currently resolving, drain in place
8. Delayed gastric emptying (grade A), postoperative — resolved

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
- Pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with regional lymphadenectomy — approximately 4 weeks ago (index procedure); included hepaticojejunostomy, pancreaticojejunostomy, gastrojejunostomy
- No prior abdominal surgeries

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired high school science teacher. Non-smoker (lifetime). No alcohol use. Lives with adult daughter who has been actively assisting with drain care and medication management at home. Strong social support. College-educated; engaged and inquisitive about her disease course. No illicit drug use.

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of pancreatic cancer. Mother had breast cancer (postmenopausal onset). Father had hypertension and coronary artery disease. No known Lynch syndrome or BRCA-related family history. Germline testing performed: no pathogenic variants in BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. Pancrelipase (PERT; Creon 36,000 units lipase) — with all meals and snacks
2. Insulin glargine (Lantus) — 18 units subcutaneously at bedtime (dose per endocrinology)
3. Insulin lispro (Humalog) — sliding scale with meals per endocrinology protocol
4. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily
5. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally nightly
6. Acetaminophen 650 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain
7. Ondansetron 4 mg orally every 8 hours as needed for nausea
8. Omeprazole 40 mg orally twice daily (post-Whipple acid suppression)
9. Low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily
10. Multivitamin daily
11. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (vitamins A, D, E, K)
12. Octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneously three times daily (for pancreatic fistula; plan to taper pending drain output/amylase assessment today)

Note: Metformin was discontinued at the time of surgery given pancreatogenic diabetes and post-surgical considerations.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

General: Reports mild fatigue, improved from the immediate post-operative period. No fevers or chills. Weight loss ongoing but decelerated. Appetite slowly improving.
Gastrointestinal: Decreased drain output over past week (patient estimated approximately 20-30 mL over the last 24 hours, down from 50-80 mL per day in the first week at home). Mild persistent loose stools 2-3 times daily; no bloody stool, no melena. Mild epigastric discomfort with large meals. Early satiety present. No nausea in past week. No vomiting.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Blood glucose elevated but trending to better control. No hypoglycemic episodes. No polydipsia or polyuria beyond baseline.
Neurologic: Residual fingertip and toe paresthesias from prior oxaliplatin exposure (grade 1-2 peripheral sensory neuropathy), stable and not worsening.
Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized deconditioning. Ambulating independently at home.
Skin/Wound: No erythema, drainage, or tenderness at the midline incision beyond expected mild sensitivity. Drain site appears clean per patient report.
Cardiopulmonary: No chest pain, dyspnea, or palpitations. No lower extremity swelling.
GU: No dysuria or hematuria.
Psychiatric: Patient denies depression or anxiety but acknowledges significant emotional adjustment to the diagnosis, surgical changes, and uncertainty about long-term prognosis. She has been speaking with a social worker from the cancer center.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Temperature 36.8°C, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 58.4 kg (BMI approximately 22.3 kg/m2; down from 62.9 kg/BMI 24.1 at diagnosis).

General: Alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. Well-appearing. Mildly cachectic appearance compared to pre-illness baseline. Conversational and engaged.

Head and Neck: Anicteric sclerae (no jaundice). Mucous membranes moist. No lymphadenopathy appreciated in the cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary chains. Oropharynx clear.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rubs.

Abdomen: Midline laparotomy incision well-healed, approximately 18 cm in length. Staples removed; steri-strips in place at superior and inferior aspects. No wound dehiscence. No surrounding erythema, warmth, or fluctuance. Mild tenderness to deep palpation at the epigastric region, expected in post-Whipple setting. Surgical drain (Jackson-Pratt) exits through the right flank; drain site clean with minimal surrounding erythema. Drain output over 24 hours: approximately 25 mL of thin, light amber fluid. Bowel sounds present throughout. No rebound tenderness or guarding. No palpable masses. No ascites appreciated by percussion or shifting dullness.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Mild distal fingertip hypesthesia bilaterally consistent with prior oxaliplatin-associated neuropathy (grade 1 at this visit). No skin breakdown.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Motor function intact. Gait steady.

Skin: No jaundice. No spider angiomata. No petechiae or ecchymoses beyond well-healing surgical site.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (OBTAINED TODAY)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.8 x 10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, improving from post-operative nadir)
- Hematocrit: 31.2%
- Platelets: 318 x 10^9/L (normal)
- ANC: 4.1 x 10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.8 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min)
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL (normal; previously mildly elevated post-op)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase: 98 U/L (normalizing)
- ALT: 32 U/L (normal)
- AST: 28 U/L (normal)
- Albumin: 3.1 g/dL (mildly low; nutritional recovery ongoing)
- Total protein: 6.2 g/dL
- Glucose (fasting): 188 mg/dL (still elevated; glycemic optimization in progress)
- HbA1c: pending (will be drawn today; prior value 9.2% at diagnosis)

Drain fluid amylase (drawn today): 128 U/L (down from >3,000 U/L on POD 3; now approaching normal serum range, consistent with resolving fistula)

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 38 U/mL (mildly above institutional upper limit of normal of 37 U/mL; trending down from 52 U/mL pre-operatively; essentially normalizing)
- CEA: 2.3 ng/mL (within normal range)

Coagulation:
- INR: 1.1 (normal)
- PT/aPTT: within normal limits

Nutritional labs:
- Prealbumin: 14 mg/dL (mildly low; improving)
- Zinc: 68 mcg/dL (low-normal; supplementation discussed)
- Vitamin D (25-OH): 22 ng/mL (insufficient; supplementation increased)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging obtained at this visit. Most recent imaging is the pre-operative restaging CT abdomen/pelvis (performed approximately 6 weeks prior to surgery), which demonstrated a 2.3 cm treated pancreatic head mass with SMA abutment estimated at 80-90 degrees and no evidence of distant metastasis. Post-operative surveillance imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast) will be planned approximately 8-10 weeks post-operatively once the pancreatic fistula has fully resolved, to establish a post-surgical baseline.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process (KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, CDKN2A deleted; MSS; TMB-low) who has completed a full course of multimodal neoadjuvant therapy (8 cycles mFOLFIRINOX, then capecitabine-based chemoradiation 50.4 Gy/28 fractions) followed by pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with R0 resection (pT3 pN1 M0, Stage IIB; 2/18 nodes positive; CAP treatment response grade 2). She is now 4 weeks post-operatively and recovering well overall, with her most notable ongoing issue being a resolving grade B pancreatic fistula.

1. PANCREATIC FISTULA (ISGPF GRADE B) — RESOLVING
   - Drain fluid amylase today is 128 U/L, markedly decreased from the post-operative peak (>3,000 U/L on POD 3) and now approaching normal serum range.
   - Drain output over the past 24 hours is approximately 25 mL of thin, non-turbid fluid — a significant reduction from prior weeks.
   - Clinical status is reassuring: no fever, no abdominal pain beyond baseline surgical tenderness, no signs of sepsis or collection formation.
   - Plan: Given drain amylase normalization and markedly reduced output, the surgical drain will be removed at today's visit. The patient and her daughter are counseled that if she develops fever, increased abdominal pain, or change in clinical status, she should contact the office or report to the emergency department for evaluation.
   - Octreotide: Given resolution of fistula evidenced by drain amylase normalization, octreotide will be discontinued today. No taper required given short-course use.
   - She will follow up with drain site wound care instructions.

2. SURGICAL PATHOLOGY DISCUSSION
   - Formal results reviewed in detail with the patient and her daughter. Key points communicated:
     - R0 (clear margin) resection was achieved — the tumor was completely removed with the closest margin being 3 mm at the SMA/uncinate margin. This is the best possible surgical outcome in this setting.
     - Pathologic treatment response (CAP grade 2) indicates meaningful but incomplete tumor kill from neoadjuvant therapy — approximately 40% viable tumor remained. This represents a moderate response, consistent with the clinical and radiographic improvements observed.
     - Two of 18 lymph nodes were found to harbor microscopic metastatic disease (pN1) — this upgrades the final pathologic stage to IIB, which carries prognostic significance but does not preclude curative intent.
     - Extensive perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion are adverse histologic features that highlight the importance of adjuvant therapy.
   - The patient was given time to process this information and asked thoughtful questions about what the node-positive result means for her long-term prognosis. We discussed that Stage IIB pancreatic cancer after R0 resection represents a meaningful opportunity for cure, particularly given the degree of treatment response observed, but that approximately 50-70% of patients experience recurrence within 2 years and that adjuvant chemotherapy is a critical next step. She expressed understanding and commitment to proceeding with adjuvant therapy.

3. PLANNING FOR ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY
   - Adjuvant chemotherapy is strongly recommended given Stage IIB disease (pN1), extensive perineural invasion, and lymphovascular invasion. The standard of care in this context (and supported by the ESPAC-4 trial) is gemcitabine plus capecitabine for 6 cycles (approximately 6 months).
   - She has received prior FOLFIRINOX-based therapy (neoadjuvant) and capecitabine-based chemoradiation. Gemcitabine plus capecitabine (gem/cape) represents an appropriate adjuvant regimen with a well-characterized toxicity profile.
   - Adjuvant chemotherapy should begin no earlier than 8-10 weeks post-operatively to allow adequate surgical recovery. Given the pancreatic fistula, her performance status, nutritional status, and drain removal today, I anticipate she will be ready to initiate adjuvant therapy in approximately 8-10 weeks (pending surgical recovery and nutritional optimization). I will coordinate closely with her medical oncologist for this transition.
   - I have communicated with her medical oncologist today regarding the plan and timing.

4. NUTRITIONAL STATUS AND PANCREATIC EXOCRINE INSUFFICIENCY
   - Albumin 3.1 g/dL and prealbumin 14 mg/dL reflect ongoing nutritional recovery. She has lost approximately 18 lbs from her pre-illness baseline.
   - Continue PERT (pancrelipase/Creon 36,000 units with all meals and snacks). If stool output or consistency remains a problem, Creon dose can be titrated upward (trial of 72,000 units lipase per meal). Referral to registered dietitian for tailored post-Whipple dietary guidance given today.
   - Continue oral nutritional supplementation between meals.
   - Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) supplementation continued. Vitamin D levels are insufficient; vitamin D3 2,000 IU daily increased to 4,000 IU daily.
   - Zinc supplementation initiated at 220 mg oral zinc sulfate daily.

5. PANCREATOGENIC DIABETES / GLYCEMIC MANAGEMENT
   - Glucose 188 mg/dL fasting today; glycemic control ongoing work in progress in the context of post-pancreatectomy endocrine insufficiency.
   - Endocrinology is co-managing. Continue current insulin regimen (glargine 18 units nightly, lispro sliding scale). She should continue home blood glucose monitoring at least twice daily.
   - Metformin is not being restarted given pancreatogenic diabetes and surgical anatomy.
   - HbA1c drawn today; results will be communicated to endocrinology.

6. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY
   - Residual grade 1 fingertip and toe paresthesias consistent with prior oxaliplatin (mFOLFIRINOX) exposure. No functional limitation at this time.
   - Will monitor over time; if worsening, neurology or pain management referral as needed.

7. WOUND CARE
   - Midline incision healing well. Steri-strips can be removed over the next 1-2 weeks; no need to reapply.
   - Drain site: dressing change instructions provided. Monitor for redness, swelling, or discharge.
   - Patient may shower normally with gentle wound cleansing.

8. SURVEILLANCE PLANNING
   - Post-surgical baseline CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast will be ordered for approximately 8-10 weeks post-operatively (i.e., after the patient has had adequate time to recover and prior to starting adjuvant chemotherapy). This is critical both to confirm no post-surgical complication and to serve as baseline for surveillance going forward.
   - Tumor markers (CA 19-9, CEA) will be followed serially. Today's CA 19-9 is 38 U/mL (nearly normal), which is very encouraging. The goal is normalization, and we will continue to monitor.

9. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND FUNCTIONAL RECOVERY
   - ECOG performance status: 1 (some symptoms but fully ambulatory and capable of self-care).
   - Patient is encouraged to gradually increase physical activity as tolerated — walking 15-20 minutes daily if possible, increasing as strength allows. No lifting >10 lbs for an additional 4 weeks.
   - Physical therapy and/or cardiac rehab-style exercise program may be offered prior to adjuvant chemotherapy initiation.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT
    - Patient has been connecting with the cancer center social worker and reports that conversations have been helpful. Continue social work involvement.
    - Discussion of support groups for pancreatic cancer survivors (e.g., Pancreatic Cancer Action Network, PanCAN patient services).
    - Advance directives discussed and already on file.

FOLLOW-UP:
- HPB Surgery: 6-8 weeks for surgical clearance prior to initiating adjuvant chemotherapy; sooner if any concern arises.
- Medical Oncology: 8-10 weeks for adjuvant chemotherapy initiation planning (coordinated with drain removal and nutritional recovery).
- Endocrinology: 3-4 weeks for glycemic management follow-up.
- Registered Dietitian: Referral placed today.
- Radiology: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast to be scheduled in approximately 8-10 weeks as post-surgical baseline.

Time spent: 55 minutes (>50% counseling and coordination of care).

Dictated by: [Attending HPB Surgical Oncologist]
Co-signed by: [Fellow/Resident]
